title-group.article-title: Heterogeneous weakly coupled polar nanoclusters enabling superior high-temperature capacitive energy storage

contrib-group.aff: 1https://ror.org/034t3zs45grid.454711.20000 0001 1942 5509School of Electronic Information and Artificial Intelligence, Shaanxi University of Science and Technology, Xi’an, Shaanxi China
2https://ror.org/034t3zs45grid.454711.20000 0001 1942 5509School of Materials Science and Engineering, Shaanxi University of Science and Technology, Xi’an, Shaanxi China

3https://ror.org/03a60m280grid.34418.3a0000 0001 0727 9022Center of Electron Microscopy, Ministry of Education Key Laboratory of Green Preparation and Application for Functional Materials, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Hubei University, Wuhan, China
4Research Center for Advanced Functional Ceramics, Wuzhen Laboratory, Jiaxing, Zhengjiang China

6https://ror.org/03cve4549grid.12527.330000 0001 0662 3178Center of Advanced Ceramic Materials and Devices, Yangtze Delta Region Institute of Tsinghua University, Jiaxing, China
7https://ror.org/03cve4549grid.12527.330000 0001 0662 3178State Key Laboratory of New Ceramic Materials, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Tsinghua University, Beijing, China

You do not have permission under this licence to share adapted material derived from this article or parts of it. The images or other third party material in this article are included in the article’s Creative Commons licence, unless indicated otherwise in a credit line to the material. To view a copy of this licence, visit http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/.

abstract.p: Ceramic capacitors, although promising for advanced high-power energy storage, face challenges in energy density and efficiency at high temperatures, which restricts their practical applications. Guided by phase field simulations, we propose a structural design strategy to construct weakly coupled polar nanoclusters in superparaelectric state, and fabricate BaTiO3-based multilayer ceramic capacitors via prototype device technology. The capacitors achieve an ultrahigh energy storage density of 19.0 J·cm-3 and a high energy storage efficiency of 95.5% at room temperature. More importantly, both metrics are still on the order of > 10.0 J·cm-3 and > 95.0%, respectively, over 25-160 oC, outperforming previously reported ceramic capacitors. The weaken coupling between adjacent nanoclusters caused by disordered polar configurations, suppresses nonlinear polarization response and temperature sensitivity, ultimately enabling superior energy storage performance, which is confirmed by atomic-scale microstructure analysis. This work advances next-generation electronics and provides insights for developing high-performance high-temperature ceramic capacitors.

Ceramic capacitors face challenges in energy storage performance at high temperatures, restricting their applications. Here the high-performance BaTiO3-based capacitors, as a paradigm, are designed by constructing weakly coupled polar nanoclusters.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Lead-free ceramic capacitors have emerged as a key component for applications in portable electronics, electric vehicles, and large-scale energy storage systems due to their significant advantages, including eco-friendliness and ultra-high power density, which enable ultra-fast charging and discharging rates, as well as high voltage tolerance and reliability1–3. However, compared to batteries and electrochemical capacitors, relatively low recoverable energy density (Wrec) and efficiency (η) limit their potential to meet practical requirements for miniaturization and integration4–6. In order to meet the requirements of practical applications, ceramic dielectric capacitors must feature extremely slim polarization-electric field (P-E) loops and high field-induced polarization7,8. However, achieving high polarization often leads to significant hysteresis effects, and vice versa9,10. In response to this challenge, researchers employed various strategies to engineer the transition from ferroelectrics (FEs) to relaxor ferroelectrics (RFEs) and have made significant progress11,12. This transition destroys the long-range ordered, strongly coupled micron-scale FE domains, resulting in lower polarization switching energy barriers and enhanced η value of ceramic capacitors13,14. According to numerous reported ceramic systems, the BaTiO3-x(Na0.5Bi0.5)TiO3 (0 ≤ x ≤ 1, BT-100xNBT) RFEs have attracted significant attention due to its significant tetragonal distortions with varying composition and high spontaneous polarization15–17. For instance, through precise local structural design in BT-50NBT-based RFEs, an exceptionally high energy density (>10 J cm−3) and energy efficiency (>90%) have been achieved18. Unfortunately, the Wrec value of this ceramic at a high temperature of 150 °C is sharply decreased to approximately 8.5 J cm−3, and the corresponding η is also reduced to around 85%. Temperature-sensitive energy storage performance and low high-temperature capacitance density (<10 J cm−3) of recently reported lead-free ceramics still restrict their applicability at high-temperature conditions. For instance, the (Bi1/3Na1/6K1/6Ba1/6Sr1/6)(Ti0.8Zr0.2)O3 high-entropy ceramic exhibits an excellent energy storage density of 18.7 J cm−3 at room temperature. However, its energy storage density notably drops below 10.0 J cm−3 at 160 °C19. Similarly, the BaTiO3-(Bi0.5Na0.5)(Zn1/3Nb2/3)O3 system formed by multi-ion doping has an ultrahigh energy storage efficiency of 96.1%, but the insufficient thermal stability of energy storage density limits its applicability in a wide temperature range20. Therefore, developing lead-free ceramic dielectric capacitors with high energy storage performance and excellent high-temperature stability is crucial for the next generation of dielectric capacitors that can operate stably under high electric fields and at temperatures up to 150 °C.

Typical RFEs always exhibit small polarization hysteresis while suffering from early polarization saturation under sufficiently high external electric fields, restricting the rapid enhancement of energy storage performance when increasing the external electric field21. Moreover, the energy storage performance of typical RFEs is often sensitive to temperature changes22. From a thermodynamic perspective, domain size plays a crucial role in influencing the polarization response, encompassing long-range FE interactions and switching barriers. When sufficiently reducing the domain size and weakening the domain coupling, the polar state becomes easily redirectable, resulting in nearly zero polarization hysteresis and mitigated polarization saturation. Interestingly, as shown in Fig. 1a, this phenomenon can be observed in superparaelectric state within the temperature range of Tm < T < TB, where Tm is the peak temperature of the dielectric constant and TB represents the Burns temperature. In comparison to strongly coupled micro-domains and nano-domains, weakly coupled polar nanoclusters in superparaelectric state exhibit a super-fast and reversible response under loading and unloading the external electric field, which prevents the phenomenon of polarization hysteresis and premature polarization saturation, resulting in remarkable energy storage density and efficiency21,23. In addition, these weakly coupled polar nanoclusters are insensitive to the variation of ambient temperature, and the corresponding dielectric constant remains stable over a broad temperature range, which is highly important for enhancing the thermal stability of energy storage performance24,25.Fig. 1Design strategy of weakly coupled polar nanoclusters in dielectrics with superparaelectric state for superior capacitive energy storage.
a Dielectric properties and domain characteristics of typical RFEs. b Evolution of crystal structure, domain, and P-E loop of RFEs from strongly coupled micro-domains to weakly coupled disordered polar nanoclusters.

Guided by phase-field simulations, as shown in Fig. 1b, the introduction of foreign ions such as Sr2+, La3+, and Zr4+ into the BT-35NBT matrix increases the local random field, disrupting long-range ordered, strongly coupled micro-domains and forming small weakly coupled polar nanoclusters. This configuration features rhombohedral (R) and tetragonal (T) polar nanoclusters embedded within cubic (C) paraelectric matrixes. Adjusting the ratio and distribution of polar and nonpolar states for R-, T-, and C-phases aims to minimize polarization hysteresis while maximizing maximum polarization. Such weakly coupled polar nanoclusters can exert a pinning effect that effectively inhibits local structural changes induced by atomic thermal vibration and thereby significantly enhances the stability of the phase structure over a wide temperature range. It is encouraging that the unique structural design enables the fabricated multilayer ceramic capacitors (MLCCs) to exhibit excellent energy storage performance not only at room temperature, but also over a wide temperature range. Even at a high temperature of 160 °C, the Wrec remains greater than 10.0 J·cm-3, and the η exceeds 95.0%. This advancement provides a significant reference for developing next-generation high-temperature dielectric capacitors.

sec.title: Results and discussion
sec.p:
To uncover the underlying origin of the enhanced high-temperature energy storage performance of lead-free ceramic dielectrics induced by weakly coupled polar nanoclusters, the two-dimensional phase field simulation was used to study the correlation between the polar structure evolution and the corresponding hysteresis behavior of (1 − x)[0.65BaTiO3-0.35(Na0.5Bi0.5)TiO3]-xSr0.7La0.2ZrO3 (SLZ100x, x = 0, 0.05, 0.1, 0.15, 0.2) ceramics at different temperatures, as shown in Fig. 2a and Supplementary Fig. 1. The initial BT-35NBT ceramic features long-range ordered, strongly coupled T-phase domain structure, as substantiated by its P-E hysteresis loops with large polarization but high hysteresis loss. After introducing SLZ, as expected, the domains were transformed from macroscopic FE domains to nanodomains and subsequently to smaller weakly coupled polar nanoclusters, accompanied by increasingly slender P-E loops. This progression suggests that the introduction of SLZ effectively disrupts the long-range FE order in BT-35NBT ceramics. Simultaneously, the content of the T-phase decreases significantly, resulting in a disordered polarization structure with coexisting R-, T- and C-phases. The randomly distributed polar structure modifies local energy barriers, stabilizes the favorable polarization, and forms the unique structures with weakly coupled R and T polar nanoclusters divided by nonpolar C phase over a broad temperature range, thereby enhancing temperature-insensitive energy storage performance. In addition, the weak coupling characteristic is responsible for excellent electric field response performance in the SLZ15 ceramic (Fig. 2b). In the initial state, benefiting from such a weak coupling effect, the polarization directions are highly randomly distributed. Upon the loading of external electric field, the polarizations rapidly reorient and align with the electric field direction. After the removal of external electric field, the polarization quickly deviates from the directional alignment state and eventually recovers its initial disordered distribution, demonstrating fast and reversible polarization response behavior. At the same time, optimizing the ratio and distribution of polar nanoclusters further reduces potential barriers for domain switching, enabling the flattest domain switching paths (Fig. 2c). Weakly coupled polar nanoclusters also promote the formation of random fields, which provides a strong driving force for the electric field-induced recovery of large domains to a multi-domain state (Fig. 2d). These results lead to a recoverable electric field-induced relaxor FE state and promote linear-like polarization behavior.Fig. 2Phase-field simulation of polar structure.
a Evolution of the domain structure in SLZx ceramics with increasing temperature. b Evolution of microscopic domain structure in the SLZ15 ceramic under a specific electric field. c Landau energy distributions and d local random field distributions for SLZ10 and SLZ15 ceramics.

An aberration-corrected scanning transmission electron microscope was employed to investigate the atomic-scale structural characteristics of SLZx ceramics. Figure 3a, b shows the high-angle annular dark-field scanning transmission electron microscopy (HAADF-STEM) images for representative compositions of BT-35NBT and SLZ15 ceramics along [100]c direction. The polarization displacement vectors were computed through software simulation and presented by yellow arrows. Herein, the morphological structure is identified by observing the projected displacements of the B-site cations (Zr/Ti) relative to the four nearest A-site cations (Ba/Na/Bi/Sr) at the lattice center. Based on the 2D Gaussian peak fitting results, the larger domains can be observed by same arrow pointing for BT-35NBT ceramics (highlighted by the green arrow at the right of Fig. 3a). By contrast, local fluctuations are observed for the SLZ15 ceramic due to the disordered nature of the A-site cations. As a result, smaller weakly coupled polar nanoclusters of R- and T-phases are embedded within nonpolar C-phase matrixes (Fig. 3b). These results confirm that SLZx ceramics with weakly coupled polar nanoclusters were successfully fabricated under the guidance of phase-field simulations. The disordered and weakly coupled polar nanoclusters contribute to enhancing the stability of local structure and energy storage performance under temperature fluctuation26. Additionally, as observed in the atomic-resolved energy-dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) mapping for the SLZ15 ceramic in Supplementary Fig. 2, the Ba, Bi, Na, La, and Sr elements are distributed in A-sites, while the Zr and Ti elements are distributed in B-sites, respectively. It promotes the destabilization of cation sites in the perovskite structure and induces the formation of weakly coupled polar nanoclusters. From the comparative distribution of pixel intensities of A- and B-site atomic columns between the BT-35NBT and SLZ15 ceramics, it can be concluded that multi-element doping significantly enhances the atomic-scale disorder of the ceramics (Fig. 3c–f). Significantly, the intensity fluctuation is more prominent at the A-site columns (Ba2+/Na+/Bi3+/Sr2+/La2+) in the SLZ15 ceramic, suggesting large random displacements of A-site cations. These random shifts directly generate strong local electric fields that disrupt long-range polar order. In contrast, the B-site (Ti4+/Zr4+) columns show small intensity variations. Nevertheless, their fluctuations exhibit high spatial correlation closely linked to cooperative displacements of B-site cations and tilting of oxygen octahedra. This correlated B-site behavior locally biases polarization directions and contributes to stabilizing nanoscale polar nanoclusters. According to the axial displacement data, the polarization angle and amplitude mapping of BT-35NBT and SLZ15 ceramics along the [100]c direction is illustrated in Supplementary Fig. 3. Compared to the BT-35NBT ceramic, the polarization angle and amplitude of the SLZ15 ceramic exhibit a markedly non-uniform random distribution. Meanwhile, this random distribution of T- and R-phase polar nanoclusters is isolated by nonpolar C-phase, facilitating a more rapid and efficient response to the external electric field27. In addition, compared with the average c/a ratio of the BT-35NBT ceramic, that of the SLZ15 ceramic displays a noticeable decrease accompanied by a more pronounced discrete distribution (Supplementary Fig. 4). The variation of the c/a ratio indicates that as SLZ content increases, atomic disorder within the system escalates and lattice distortion intensifies, further enhancing the random field and effectively delaying polarization saturation28.Fig. 3Microstructure characteristics of the BT-35NBT and SLZ15 ceramics.
Atomic-resolution HAADF STEM image of polarization vector along the [100]c direction for a BT-35NBT and b SLZ15 ceramics. Yellow arrows indicate the polarization vectors from the center B-site cations to the corner A-site cations. Pixel integrated intensity at A-site of c BT-35NBT and e SLZ15 ceramics as well as pixel integrated intensity at A-site of d BT-35NBT and f SLZ15 ceramics along [100]c.

X-ray diffraction (XRD, Supplementary Fig. 5) analysis confirms that all samples exhibit a pure perovskite phase. The pure BT-35NBT ceramic displays a characteristic T phase, evident from the distinct splitting of XRD peaks (002)/(200)28,29. As the SLZ content increases, the (200) diffraction peak gradually shifts to a low angle, primarily due to the larger radius of the dopant ions (R(Sr2+) = 1.44 Å, R(La3+) = 1.06 Å, R(Zr4+) = 0.79 Å), which results in lattice expansion. To further analyze the phase composition in the SLZx ceramics, Rietveld refinement was performed on the XRD data for each composition, as shown in Fig. 4a and Supplementary Fig. 6. The results indicate that the rhombohedral (R3c) and tetragonal (P4bm) phases coexist in the SLZ15 ceramic, with mass fractions of 56.2 wt% and 43.8 wt%, respectively. Notably, as the SLZ content increases, the proportion of the R-phase at room temperature increases monotonically, from 24.6 wt% at x = 0.05–71.4 wt% at x = 0.20. Both the R-phase and T-phase are polar phases with high polarization, which facilitates the achievement of a larger maximum polarization (Pmax) in ceramic dielectrics under the application of an external electric field23.Fig. 4Phase structure and electrical performance of the SLZx ceramics.
a Fitted XRD pattern of the SLZ15 ceramic. b Phase diagram of SLZx ceramics as a function of temperature and SLZ content. c Evolution of FORC distributions for the SLZ15 ceramic. d Energy storage performance of SLZx ceramics at critical electric fields. e Energy storage performance of SLZ15-RRP ceramics under various electric fields. f Electric potential distribution and electrical tree evolution in BT-35NBT and SLZ15-RRP ceramics. Energy density and efficiency of SLZ15-RRP ceramics as a function of g temperature and h cycling number at 500 kV cm−1. i Time dependence of discharge energy density of the SLZ15-RRP ceramic at different temperatures.

To primarily confirm the designed SLZx ceramics in superparaelectric state near room temperature, the temperature dependence of dielectric constant and dielectric loss (tanδ) was analyzed (Supplementary Figs. 7 and 8). As the SLZ content increases, the tanδ exhibits a significant decreasing trend while remaining consistently below 0.01 across a broad temperature range from room temperature to 300 °C, accompanied by the shift of Tm towards low temperatures from 184 °C for x = 0 to −83 °C for x = 0.20 at 1 kHz. Additionally, the relaxor diffusion factor γ, calculated using the modified Curie-Weiss law30, tends to increase with the increment of x, indicating an improvement in relaxor properties (Supplementary Fig. 9). Based on the measured dielectric spectrum at 1 kHz, a possible composition-temperature phase diagram was constructed (Fig. 4b). The Tm significantly decreases with increasing SLZ content, facilitating the transition from relaxor FE to superparaelectric state. Consequently, the designed ceramics are fully situated in superparaelectric state from −56 °C to 162 °C when x = 0.15, indicating the existence of weakly coupled polar nanoclusters over a broad temperature range. In addition, weakened coupling between the R-phase and T-phase polar nanoclusters significantly reduces the stress required for polarization reorientation in response to an external electric field, leading to lower strain vibration and potential failure risks8,31.

The relaxation characteristics of SLZx ceramics were studied through the first-order reversal curve (FORC) distribution, as shown in Fig. 4c and Supplementary Fig. 10. The BT-35NBT ceramic displays a high-intensity distribution near the origin, indicative of strongly coupled FE domain switching at low electric fields, resulting in an overall increase in polarization32. This inhomogeneous FORC intensity distribution reflects the typical characteristics of strong polarization nonlinearity in FE materials33. In contrast, the FORC intensity of the SLZ15 ceramic is more uniform distributed across all electric fields, indicating decreased coercive fields, reduced FE nonlinearity, and a more dispersed polarization contribution34. In addition, the representative samples of BT-35NBT and SLZ15 ceramics were selected to analyze further the dynamic response of domains by piezoelectric force microscopy (PFM) at various voltages and relaxation times (Supplementary Figs. 11 and 12). For the BT-35NBT ceramic, a strong domain-switching signal is observed at a low voltage of 10 V, whereas the domain-switching signal in the SLZ15 ceramic is weak at this voltage but becomes more pronounced with increasing voltage, suggesting that local random fields and highly dynamic polar nanoclusters are present in the SLZ15 ceramic. Weakly coupled polar nanoclusters require a higher electric field to achieve long-range polarization, which explains the reduced polarization saturation observed in the SLZ15 ceramic. Furthermore, after removing the voltage and allowing a relaxation time of 20 min, the BT-35NBT ceramic retains their original remarkable signals, while the domain-switching signals in the SLZ15 ceramic are barely detectable. These results indicate that the introduction of SLZ diminishes the domain size of the BT-35NBT ceramic and induces weakly coupled polar nanoclusters. This structural modulation yields negligible Pr by virtue of rapid reversible polarization response, while concurrently enhancing the overall energy storage performance.

For ceramic capacitors, the breakdown strength (Eb) significantly influences their operating electric field and energy storage density. Thus, the breakdown characteristics of SLZx ceramics were initially evaluated using the Weibull distribution function35. All samples exhibit a linear relationship with a large shape parameter (β > 10, Supplementary Fig. 13), indicating the reliability of the results and consistent quality of the ceramic samples. In fact, the Eb of ceramic dielectrics is influenced by various complex factors, such as band gap (Eg), grain size, and insulation properties36,37. For SLZx ceramics, the Eg is significantly enhanced with increasing the SLZ content (Supplementary Fig. 14). Specifically, the values of Eg are enhanced monotonously from 3.12 eV for the SLZ5 ceramic to 3.18 eV for the SLZ20 ceramic, leading to a substantial increase in the Eb. In addition, the average grain size (Gₐ) and Eb exhibit a negative correlation in ceramic dielectrics28,38. According to the measured SEM (Supplementary Fig. 15), the Ga was evaluated by Nano Measurer software. The Ga exhibits a decreasing tendency, from 0.72 μm for the BT-35NBT ceramic to 0.43 μm for the SLZ20 ceramic. The smaller Ga is beneficial to achieving higher Eb. Despite the fact that the radius of impedance spectra for SLZx ceramics tends to decrease with increasing temperature, the higher SLZ content still contributes to a larger radius, indicating the enhancement of total resistivity and Eb at high temperatures (Supplementary Fig. 16). In addition, the distinct single peaks of Z” and M” can be observed for the BT-35NBT ceramic, but they do not coincide at the same frequency (Supplementary Fig. 17). This lack of overlap indicates electrical inhomogeneity. In contrast, the peaks of Z” and M” closely overlap at the same frequency for the SLZ15 ceramic, demonstrating that the introduction of SLZ into the BT-35NBT ceramic effectively improves the electrical homogeneity. This enhancement in electrical homogeneity also contributes to the increase in Eb39.

To better evaluate the overall energy storage performance of the designed ceramics, the unipolar P-E loops were measured at their critical electric field (Supplementary Figs. 18 and 19), and the related capacitive energy storage performance of Wrec and η were calculated (Fig. 4d). Despite the Pmax of the BT-35NBT ceramic reaching 37.9 μC cm−2 at a low breakdown strength (Eb = 160 kV cm−1), while the remnant polarization (Pr) is as high as 23.8 μC cm−2, leading to low energy storage density and efficiency (Wrec = 1.7 J cm−3, η = 48.6%, Supplementary Fig. 18). By tailoring the polar nanocluster configuration, the values of Wrec and η were dramatically improved to be on the order of 9.2 ± 0.28 J cm−3 and 91.6 ± 0.24% at x = 0.15. It is due to the fact that the strongly coupled FE order of the BT-35NBT ceramic is disrupted, resulting in the formation of R- and T-phase weakly coupled polar nanoclusters isolated by nonpolar C-phase matrixes, which contributes to the rapid polarization response and slim P-E loops. Nevertheless, when excessive SLZ is introduced (e.g., at x = 0.2), the formation of nonpolar C-phase becomes dominant. Consequently, the Pmax value at an electric field of 620 kV cm−1 drops to only 30.5 μC cm−2, and the Wrec ultimately decreases to 7.5 J cm−3. These results indicate that the generation of weakly coupled polar nanoclusters and the alterations in nanocluster configuration significantly optimize the energy storage performance of lead-free ceramics. Compared to the traditional solid-state reaction process, the method of repeated rolling process (RRP) facilitates the refinement of grain size and enhances sintering uniformity, leading to the increased Eb of ceramic dielectrics25,40. Therefore, SLZ15 ceramics were prepared via the RRP (SLZ15-RRP) using SLZ15 powder to further optimize their energy storage performance. Figure 4e illustrates the unipolar P-E loops of the SLZ15-RRP ceramic under different electric fields. Obviously, the Pr remains negligible and its value is as low as 0.9 μC cm−2 even at 780 kV cm−1. It is worth noting that an ultra-high Wrec of 13.6 J cm−3, along with a near-ideal efficiency of 95.0%, can be achieved for the SLZ15-RRP ceramic. The reliability of the Eb is validated through Weibull distribution analysis, as shown in Supplementary Fig. 20. The β of the SLZ15-RRP ceramic is 17.34, indicating high reliability of the Eb. The calculated Eb is on the order of 782.9 kV cm−1, closely corresponding to the Eb obtained in P-E loops. Herein, the remarkable comprehensive energy storage performance in this study is primarily due to their weakly coupled polar nanoclusters, compact microstructure, and uniform and small grain size (Supplementary Fig. 21). Weakly coupled polar nanoclusters exhibit high sensitivity to external electric fields, which enables ceramic dielectrics to achieve near-ideal energy efficiency. Meanwhile, the refined grain size effectively inhibits electrical tree propagation and shortens their growth paths, thereby significantly enhancing the Eb, as shown in Fig. 4f and Supplementary Fig. 22.

Except for achieving high energy storage performance at room temperature, their stability under different conditions is also critical in practical applications. Therefore, over a broad temperature range from room temperature to 160 °C, the P-E loops consistently maintain a slim shape and the variation of Wrec and η is less than 10.0% and 5.0%, respectively, at an electric field of 500 kV cm−1 (Fig. 4g and Supplementary Fig. 23). To further assess frequency adaptability, P-E loops of the SLZ15-RRP ceramic were tested over a frequency range of 1–100 Hz (Supplementary Fig. 24). Obviously, the Wrec remains stably at ~ 7.9 J cm−3, and the η consistently stays above 94.0%, which indicates excellent frequency stability. Moreover, the energy storage performance of the SLZ15-RRP ceramic remains stable with a change rate of less than 2.0% in both Wrec and η after 106 cycles (Fig. 4h and Supplementary Fig. 25). In addition, the overdamped discharge characteristics of the SLZ15-RRP ceramic were investigated using a specially designed circuit with a 100 Ω resistor at various temperatures under an electric field of 500 kV cm−1. The overdamped oscillation waveforms and discharge energy storage performance are displayed in Supplementary Fig. 26. In a high-temperature environment, the discharged energy density (Wd) can still maintain over 90.0% of this value under the same voltage conditions. The stable Wd of ~ 6.0 J cm−3 and fast discharge time (t0.9) of ~ 44 ns also indicate the thermally stable discharge performance (Fig. 4i). These results demonstrate the promising potential of the SLZ15-RRP ceramic for high-temperature energy storage applications.

The MLCCs, which were fabricated with five effective dielectric layers using SLZ15 ceramic powder, exhibit a well-defined layered structure and sharp dielectric-electrode interfaces, as shown in the cross-sectional microstructure (Fig. 5a). Evidently, no discernible elemental interdiffusion is observed between the dielectric and electrode layers, indicating excellent chemical compatibility during co-firing. During the process of steadily increasing electric field up to breakdown field, the SLZ15-MLCCs still displays an extremely slim P-E loops, as show in Fig. 5b. The Pmax increases steadily, whereas the Pr remains nearly a constant (below 0.9 μC cm−2). As the electric field increases steadily, it allows the SLZ15-MLCCs to attain the Wrec of 19.0 J cm−3 at 1050 kV cm−1, and the η remains remarkably high (~95.5%), as depicted in Fig. 5c, which is notably superior to most of the current reported lead-free MLCCs (Fig. 5d and Supplementary Table 1)28,41–49. To evaluate practical reliability, cyclic stability tests were conducted under 700 kV cm−1. After 106 cycles, neither the Wrec nor the η exhibits significant degradation (Fig. 5e and Supplementary Fig. 27), which confirms that the excellent cycling endurance is suitable for long-term operation. Notably, the SLZ15-MLCCs exhibit excellent stability over broad ranges of frequency and temperature. The variations in its P-E loops are negligible over a temperature range of 25–160 °C and a frequency range of 1–100 Hz (Supplementary Figs. 28 and 29a). Meanwhile, it maintains a Wrec of over 10.0 J cm−3 and an η of over 95.0% within these ranges, with the variation rate of the energy storage performance being less than 6.0% (Fig. 5f and Supplementary Fig. 29b). The excellent cycling, frequency, and temperature stability of the SLZ15-MLCCs fully confirms their significant potential for practical deployment.Fig. 5Energy storage performance of the SLZ15-MLCCs.
a Cross-sectional SEM image of the designed SLZ15-MLCCs and the corresponding element distribution. The top-right inset displays a digital photograph of the SLZ15-MLCCs. b P-E loops of the SLZ15-MLCCs. c Wrec and η as a function of electric field up to Eb. d Comparison of the energy storage properties of the SLZ15-MLCCs with those of the state-of-the-art MLCCs. e Cycle number-dependent Wrec and η under 700 kV cm−1. f Wrec and η at 700 kV cm−1 as a function of temperature. g Calculated P-E loops of the SLZ15-MLCCs at different temperatures. Temperature evolution of h XRD patterns and i Raman spectra for the SLZ15 ceramic from 25 to 200 °C.

It is well known that ceramic dielectric capacitors exhibit excellent energy storage performance at high temperatures, closely linked to variations in their crystal structures50. As the temperature increases, the simulated P-E loops in Fig. 5g show a slight decrease in Pmax, which is consistent with the above experimental results. This trend can be attributed to the gradual reduction in domain size and dielectric constant with rising temperature. Furthermore, the structural characteristics of the SLZ15 ceramic were evaluated using XRD and Raman spectroscopy from 25 °C to 200 °C, as shown in Fig. 5h, i and Supplementary Fig. 30. The number and relative intensity of XRD diffraction peaks remain consistent across the temperature range, and the (111) and (200) diffraction peaks shift towards lower angles due to thermal expansion without splitting, indicating the stability of phase structure. Meanwhile, as shown in Fig. 5h, the SLZ15 ceramic exhibits nearly diffuse Raman patterns with consistent peaks over a broad temperature range. These results suggest that the crystal structure remains stable within the tested temperature range, providing the structural characteristics of excellent high-temperature energy storage performance.

In summary, guided by phase-field simulations, this work presents an effective strategy for enhancing the capacitive energy storage of lead-free ceramics by designing weakly coupled polar nanoclusters in superparaelectric state. Atomic-scale HAADF-STEM and polarization mapping reveal nanostructured polar clusters with R- and T-phases that are embedded within a nonpolar C-phase matrix, forming weakly coupled polar nanoclusters. As a result, the SLZ15-MLCCs exhibit large Pmax, high applied electric field and negligible polarization hysteresis, leading to ultra-high energy storage density (Wrec ≈ 19.0 J cm−3) and near-ideal efficiency of 95.5% at room temperature. In addition, the weakly coupled and disordered polar nanoclusters remain stable at thermal fluctuations, ensuring remarkable high-temperature energy storage performance. When the temperature rises to 160 °C, its Wrec remains above 10.0 J cm−3, and the η reaches as high as over 95.0%. Additionally, the SLZ15-MLCCs also exhibit excellent frequency stability and cyclic stability, and the variations in Wrec and η both remain less than 5.0%. This work establishes a paradigm for achieving ultra-high comprehensive energy storage performance, addressing the critical need for dielectric capacitors in high-temperature environments.

sec.title: Sample preparation
sec.p: The (1-x)[0.65BaTiO3-0.35(Na0.5Bi0.5)TiO3]-xSr0.7La0.2ZrO3 system (x = 0, 0.05, 0.1, 0.15, 0.2) was fabricated by raw powders of BaCO3 (99.9%), TiO2 (98.0%), Na2CO3 (99.8%), Bi2O3 (99.0%), SrCO3 (99.0%), La2O3 (99.99%), and ZrO2 (99.0%) through a conventional solid-state reaction. The weighed raw materials were ball-milled for 24 h with ethanol, dried at 100 °C for 3 h, and subsequently calcined at 850 °C for 4 h. The calcined powders were further ball-milled for 24 h with ethanol. After drying, the powders were pre-pressed into 12 mm diameter pellets for cold isostatic pressing. The samples can be obtained by sintering at 1200–1270 °C for 4 h.

sec.title: Repeated rolling processing
sec.p: The SLZ15-RRP ceramic was fabricated by a repeated rolling processing (RRP) technique, employing pre-sintered SLZ15 powder as the raw material. The pre-sintered SLZ15 powder was initially subjected to ball-milling to refine its particle size and then homogeneously mixed with an organic binder (e.g., PVA) at a mass ratio of 2:1 (powder to binder). The mixture was subjected to 1000 rolling cycles at room temperature under a pressure of 10 MPa to form uniform ceramic tapes. These tapes were then punched into discs (12 mm diameter) and calcined at 650 °C for 12 h to remove the binder. Finally, the discs were embedded in presintered powder of the same composition and sintered at 1165 °C for 2 h using a double-crucible configuration.

sec.title: MLCCs fabrication
sec.p: The SLZ15 ceramic powder with optimal performance was selected as the raw material and thoroughly mixed with an organic vehicle composed of methyl ethyl ketone, butyl benzyl phthalate, polypropylene carbonate, and triolein. After dispersion using a high-speed homogenizer, a uniform and stable ceramic slurry was prepared. Subsequently, the slurry was cast into smooth and continuous green ceramic tapes via a tape casting machine. After drying, platinum paste was coated on the surface of the green tapes as internal electrodes by screen printing. The green tapes with internal electrodes were cut into sheets of specified size, stacked in an alternating alignment mode of internal electrodes, and dense lamination was achieved through isostatic pressing technology. Finally, the laminated green body was sintered at 1220 °C in an air atmosphere for 3 h to obtain the SLZ15-MLCCs. After the external electrodes were encapsulated on the SLZ15-MLCCs by silver paste coating, the electrical performance tests were conducted. The overlapping area of two adjacent platinum electrodes in the sintered SLZ15-MLCCs was measured to be 1.7 × 1.6 mm2, and the average thickness of dielectric layer was approximately 8 μm.

sec.title: Structure characterizations
sec.p: The phase structure was measured using X-ray diffraction (XRD, D8 Advanced, Bruker, Germany) with Cu Kα radiation. Raman spectra were obtained using a Raman scattering spectrometer (HR800, HORIBA Jobin Yvon, France). The micromorphology of the samples was recorded with a field-emission scanning electron microscope (SEM, S-4800, Hitachi, Japan), and the grain size was evaluated using a combination of the Nano Measurer software. An atomic-resolution high-angle annular dark-field scanning transmission electron microscope (HAADF-STEM, Titan Themis G2, FEI, USA) was employed to investigate the polar nanoclusters in the ceramics.

sec.title: Electric property measurements
sec.p: The P-E loops reported in this study were measured using a FE analyzer (Premier II, Radiant, USA) with a bipolar triangular waveform. The charge/discharge performance of samples was evaluated using a commercial charge-discharge device (CFD-001, Gogo Instruments Technology, China). The dynamic response of the domains to external electric fields was examined using piezoelectric force microscopy (PFM, MFP-3D, Asylum Research, USA).

sec.title: Phase-field simulations
sec.p: The details of the phase-field simulation regarding the domain structure and polarization configuration are provided in the Supplementary Information.

fn.p: Publisher’s note Springer Nature remains neutral with regard to jurisdictional claims in published maps and institutional affiliations.
These authors contributed equally: Qibin Yuan, Binglong Zheng, Ying Lin.

sec.title: Supplementary information
sec.p: The online version contains supplementary material available at 10.1038/s41467-026-69631-6.

ack.title: Acknowledgements
ack.p: This work is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 52172117, 52372116, 12404113, 12474213, 52572139, ad 12474213), the Key Research and Development Program of Shaanxi Province (Grant No. 2022GY-347), and the Shaanxi Fundamental Science Research Project for Mathematics and Physics (Grant No. 22JSQ042).

notes.title: Author contributions
notes.p: Q.B. Yuan and Z.B. Zheng conceived and designed the experiments. Q.B. Yuan, Z.B. Zheng, and Y. Lin carried out the experiments and Electric property measurements. T. Lei and W. Gong performed the phase-field simulations. H.B. Yang supervised the simulation work. J.M. Guo carried out the TEM experiments. F.-Z. Yao and K. Wang supervised the TEM experiments. Q. B. Yuan and Z. B. Zheng drafted the manuscript. H.B. Yang, F.-Z. Yao, and Y. Niu revised the manuscript. All authors discussed the results on the manuscript.

sec.title: Peer review information
sec.p: Nature Communications thanks Hitesh Borkar and the other anonymous reviewer(s) for their contribution to the peer review of this work. A peer review file is available.

notes.title: Data availability
notes.p: All data supporting this study and its findings are available within the article and its Supplementary Information. The data corresponding to this study are available from the first author and the corresponding authors upon request.

notes.title: Competing interests
notes.p: The authors declare no competing interests.
